coredata.dc:title: Phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) from dextrose for low-concentration ferric ions sensing in water
coredata.dc:creator: Molkenova, Anara
Atabaev, Timur Sh.
coredata.dc:description: The development of low-cost optical nanosensors for quick and selective metals detection in water is of high demand. In this study, we report on the fast and low-cost fabrication of phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) for potential low-concentration detection of ferric ions in water. Prepared P-CDs have a narrow size distribution of 2.8 ± 0.94 nm and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. The maximum emission was detected at 452 nm with a quantum yield of 6.73 ± 0.04%. Selectivity test reveals that prepared P-CDs are highly selective to ferric ions in water. We showed that P-CDs can be utilized as a fluorescent nanosensor for sensitive detection of Fe(III) ions in the range of 1–20 ppm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phosphorus doping
Fluorescence
Fe(III) sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), and XPS analysis (b) of P-CDs.
(a) Excitation and excitation-dependent emission curves, and (b) pH-dependent optical stability of prepared P-CDs.
Selectivity of P-CDs towards various metal ions in water.
a) Emission curves of P-CDs, and b) F/F0 plot vs. different Fe(III) concentrations in aqueous solution.
F/F0 plot vs. Fe(III) at concentrations in the range of 1–20 ppm.

head.ce:title: Phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) from dextrose for low-concentration ferric ions sensing in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science and Technology, Nazarbayev University, Astana, Kazakhstan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of low-cost optical nanosensors for quick and selective metals detection in water is of high demand. In this study, we report on the fast and low-cost fabrication of phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) for potential low-concentration detection of ferric ions in water. Prepared P-CDs have a narrow size distribution of 2.8 ± 0.94 nm and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. The maximum emission was detected at 452 nm with a quantum yield of 6.73 ± 0.04%. Selectivity test reveals that prepared P-CDs are highly selective to ferric ions in water. We showed that P-CDs can be utilized as a fluorescent nanosensor for sensitive detection of Fe(III) ions in the range of 1–20 ppm.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic metal-ion pollutants in water including copper, lead, iron, etc., pose a significant threat to human health and the environment [1,2]. Among them, iron is the most common pollutant due to natural abundance in the Earth’s crust, and its toxicity causes oxidative damage to essential cellular components (DNA, lipids, proteins, etc.) [3]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a technically simple, rapid, inexpensive and sensitive sensing solution for detecting them at low concentrations in environmental samples. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn much attention owing to their potential applications as chemosensors [4], bio-sensors [5], cell imaging [6], and catalysis [7]. Compared with conventional quantum dots or organic fluorescent molecules, CDs have considerable advantages, such as environmental friendliness, strong fluorescence, excellent chemical stability and nontoxicity [8]. Doping of CDs with phosphorous could introduce defect sites into the carbon structure, providing potential active sites to enrich their optical and chemical activities [9]. Numerous synthetic strategies, such as laser ablation, microwave, combustion/thermal methods, and electrochemical oxidation have been extensively explored to prepare CDs [10]. However, these methods suffer from several limitations, such as long and tedious (4–12 h) preparation process and the use of corrosive liquids (bases/acids) [11]. Therefore, searching for a simple and rapid synthesis method of CDs that can overcome the above mentioned limitations is highly desirable. In this study, we report a green, low cost and rapid (1 h) hydrothermal synthesis method for the preparation of phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) by mixing of dextrose and disodium hydrogen phosphate. Prepared P-CDs exhibit strong excitation-dependent fluorescence with a maximum emission at 452 nm. We found that the fluorescent properties of the P-CDs are promising for selective and sensitive detection of Fe (III) pollutant in water.

Dextrose (≥ 99.5%), Na2HPO4 (≥ 99.0%), NaCl (≥ 99.5%), KCl (≥ 99.0%), CaCl2 (≥ 97.0%), MgCl2 (≥ 98.0%), ZnCl2 (≥ 98.0%), CuCl2 × 2H2O (≥ 99.0%), SnCl2 (98.0%), FeCl2 (98.0%), FeCl3 (97.0%), and Pb(NO3)2 (≥ 99.0%) were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. For the preparation of P-CDs, 1 g of dextrose and 0.1 g of Na2HPO4 were dissolved in 25 ml of deionized (DI) water in Erlenmeyer flask with screw cap (50 ml). Closed flask with a solution was heated to 200 °C under the vigorous stirring for 1 h until the dark-brown solution formed. The prepared solution was passed through the syringe filter (0.1 μm) to remove the large aggregates. The obtained solution of P-CDs was stored at room temperature. For selectivity measurements, 100 μl of P-CDs solution was added to 1 ml of the aqueous solution of metal ions with 150 ppm concentration. For sensitivity measurements, 100 μl of P-CDs solution was added to 1 ml of Fe(III) aqueous solution with different concentrations.

The transmission electron microscopy measurements were carried out using JEM2010 F (TEM). The TEM specimen was prepared by drop casting the P-CDs water dispersion onto a carbon-coated copper grid and drying at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a VersaProbe XPS microprobe. The fluorescence and sensing properties of P-CDs were measured using an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescent spectrophotometer (FS). Quantum yield (QY) was measured by C9920-02 Hamamatsu absolute quantum yield measurement system.

The fabrication method of P-CDs was based on cheap, green and rapid hydrothermal approach. The TEM analysis provided insights into the morphology of the sample, which is shown in Fig. 1 a. One can see that the prepared P-CDs have a spherical shape with an average diameter of 2.8 ± 0.94 nm (based on TEM image analysis). Next, the XPS spectrum was obtained to identify the composition of the sample. The surface survey spectrum of P-CDs presented in Fig. 1b contains characteristic peaks of oxygen (542.5 eV), carbon (295 eV), and phosphorous (112.5 eV), confirming that the sample consists of O, C, and P. These results suggest that phosphorous dopant (1.6 at. %) is present in P-CDs.

The optical properties of the prepared P-CDs dispersed in water are shown in Fig. 2 a. FS spectra exhibit a typical excitation curve and excitation-dependent emission of the P-CDs. It is a common phenomenon that emission peaks of P-CDs shifted to longer wavelengths with increasing excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence emission peak maximum centered at 452 nm was observed upon 350 nm excitation, implying a good agreement with the excitation curve. Subsequently, the 350 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for further analysis. The absolute QY of prepared P-CDs was found to be 6.73 ± 0.04. To confirm the fluorescence stability of P-CDs, the effects of various pH values was investigated and the obtained dependence curve is shown in Fig. 2b. One can see that fluorescence emission intensity (at 452 nm) was stable over a wide pH range from 3 to 10, indicating high optical stability of P-CDs.

The selectivity of the P-CDs for various metal ions was investigated. Fig. 3 shows the changes of the fluorescence emission intensity of P-CDs occurred with potential metal ion pollutants (such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Pb2+ at a concentration of 150 ppm). A fluorescence quenching was monitored at 452 nm. Fig. 3 shows that the Fe3+ ion caused a remarkable fluorescence quenching (about 87%) of P-CDs compared to other metal ions, implying a promising application of P-CDs in selective detection of ferric ions in water samples. The observed fluorescence quenching of P-CDs in the presence of ferric ions occurred thanks to nonradiative recombination of excited electrons with unpaired d electron of Fe(III) ions [12,13].

Next, we explored the sensitivity of P-CDs to Fe(III) ions in aqueous solution. Fig. 4 a shows the change in the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs (with fixed concentration) occurred by adding various concentrations of Fe(III) ion. It is obvious that the fluorescence intensity decreased with an increase of the Fe(III) concentration from 1 to 70 ppm. Fig. 4b shows a relationship between F/F0 vs. different Fe(III) concentrations, where F is the intensity at 452 nm in the presence of various Fe(III) concentrations, and F0 is the intensity at 452 nm of the blank solution. Fig. 4b reveals the linear regression trend at low concentrations (1–20 ppm), while strong fluorescence quenching was observed at higher Fe(III) concentrations. Fig. 5 shows that the emission intensity of P-CDs decreases linearly in the range of 1–20 ppm with the correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.987. This indicates that the P-CDs have very high sensitivity towards Fe(III) ion in water. The sensing test was conducted several times with P-CDs and only negligible deviation was observed. The obtained results clearly demonstrate the reliability of P-CDs as an excellent candidate for the Fe(III) ion detection in water samples at low concentrations of 1–20 ppm. As previously noted, the employed synthesis method is green, cheap and energy efficient compared to the other reported methods.

In summary, green synthesis method was utilized to prepare fluorescent P-CDs using dextrose and disodium hydrogen phosphate. Prepared P-CDs have a narrow size distribution of 2.8 ± 0.94 nm and excellent fluorescent properties. We showed that prepared P-CDs can be utilized as selective and sensitive nanosensor towards Fe(III) ions in the range of 1–20 ppm. Thus, P-CDs demonstrate great potential for monitoring of Fe(III) pollutant at low concentrations in drinking water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Anara Molkenova would like to acknowledge the NU postdoc program.
